problem.title: Alternating Toll
problem.statement:
A road has n toll sensors numbered from 1 to n. Sensor i contributes value a_i.
For a query [l, r], define the toll balance as:
a_l - a_{l+1} + a_{l+2} - a_{l+3} + ...
where the sign always starts with plus at position l.
Answer q independent queries.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
Next q lines: l r
problem.output_spec: For each query, output the toll balance of segment [l, r].

response.why_too_slow: In the worst case every query scans O(n) elements, so q = n = 2e5 leads to O(nq) time.
response.optimization_idea: Precompute a globally alternating prefix sum anchored at index 1, then answer each query with one subtraction plus an optional negation based on the parity of l.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Alternating Toll
problem.statement:
A road has n toll sensors numbered from 1 to n. Sensor i contributes value a_i.
For a query [l, r], define the toll balance as:
a_l - a_{l+1} + a_{l+2} - a_{l+3} + ...
where the sign always starts with plus at position l.
Answer q independent queries.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
Next q lines: l r
problem.output_spec: For each query, output the toll balance of segment [l, r].

diagnosis: The raw prefix subtraction uses alternating signs anchored at index 1.
That raw value must be negated only when l is even, because then the query-defined sign pattern starts opposite to the global one.
response.fix_explanation: Change the parity check so the post-processing negation happens exactly for even l.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Alternating Toll
problem.statement:
A road has n toll sensors numbered from 1 to n. Sensor i contributes value a_i.
For a query [l, r], define the toll balance as:
a_l - a_{l+1} + a_{l+2} - a_{l+3} + ...
where the sign always starts with plus at position l.
Answer q independent queries.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
Next q lines: l r
problem.output_spec: For each query, output the toll balance of segment [l, r].

response.core_idea: Store a globally alternating prefix sum. A query becomes one prefix subtraction, and parity of l decides whether to negate it.
algorithm_steps: Build pref[i] = pref[i-1] + a_i if i is odd, otherwise pref[i-1] - a_i.
For query [l, r], compute raw = pref[r] - pref[l-1].
If l is even, negate raw because the query must start with plus at l.
invariant_or_correctness: pref[i] equals a1-a2+a3-... with signs anchored at index 1.
Subtracting pref[l-1] removes the prefix before l, leaving the alternating sum on [l, r] under the global sign pattern.
When l is even, the global sign pattern is reversed relative to the query definition, so one negation fixes it.
response.explanation: A fixed signed prefix array is enough. The only subtle point is that query signs are anchored at l, not at 1, so even starting indices need one final negation.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Balanced Pairing
problem.statement:
There are n players with skill values s_1 ... s_n, and n is even.
You must split them into n/2 pairs.
The cost of a pair is the absolute difference of the two skills.
Minimize the maximum pair cost over all chosen pairs.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Second line: n integers s_1 ... s_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the minimum possible value of the maximum pair cost.

response.core_idea: After sorting the skills, pairing adjacent players minimizes the worst pair difference.
algorithm_steps: Sort the skill array.
Pair positions (1,2), (3,4), ..., (n-1,n).
Take the maximum difference among these adjacent pairs.
invariant_or_correctness: In sorted order, any crossing pairing can be uncrossed without increasing the larger involved gap.
Repeatedly uncrossing transforms an optimal solution into one with adjacent pairs only.
response.explanation: Sorting exposes the structure of the optimal matching. Once players are ordered, adjacent pairing is forced by the exchange argument.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Rain Schedule
problem.statement:
There are n dry fields arranged in a line, initially all with water level 0.
You are given q rain operations. Operation (l, r, x) adds x units of water
to every field from l to r inclusive.
Output the final water level of every field.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Next q lines: l r x
problem.output_spec: Output n integers: the final water levels of fields 1..n.

response.why_too_slow: Touching every index of every updated segment is too expensive when both n and q are large.
response.optimization_idea: Convert every update into two difference-array boundary operations and reconstruct the final array with one prefix pass.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Rain Schedule
problem.statement:
There are n dry fields arranged in a line, initially all with water level 0.
You are given q rain operations. Operation (l, r, x) adds x units of water
to every field from l to r inclusive.
Output the final water level of every field.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Next q lines: l r x
problem.output_spec: Output n integers: the final water levels of fields 1..n.

diagnosis: In a difference array, an update on [l, r] adds at l and subtracts at r+1.
Subtracting at r removes the contribution one position too early.
response.fix_explanation: Write the negative boundary marker to diff[r+1] instead of diff[r].

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Rain Schedule
problem.statement:
There are n dry fields arranged in a line, initially all with water level 0.
You are given q rain operations. Operation (l, r, x) adds x units of water
to every field from l to r inclusive.
Output the final water level of every field.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Next q lines: l r x
problem.output_spec: Output n integers: the final water levels of fields 1..n.

response.core_idea: Mark where each range update starts and stops, then recover final values with one prefix pass.
algorithm_steps: Create a difference array diff of size n+2 initialized to 0.
For each update (l, r, x), add x to diff[l] and subtract x from diff[r+1].
Prefix-sum diff to obtain the final water levels.
invariant_or_correctness: Each update contributes x starting at l and contributes -x starting at r+1.
After prefixing, those contributions remain exactly on [l, r] and cancel elsewhere.
response.explanation: Range updates are expensive only if applied cell by cell. The difference array stores update boundaries, and one prefix pass materializes the result.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Budgeted Sprint
problem.statement:
A runner has energy costs a_1 ... a_n for consecutive track segments.
All costs are positive.
The runner wants a contiguous segment whose total cost is at most k.
Find the maximum possible length of such a segment.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n positive integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum valid segment length.

response.why_too_slow: All O(n^2) subarrays are checked explicitly.
response.optimization_idea: Because all numbers are positive, two pointers maintain the longest feasible window without ever moving either pointer backward.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Budgeted Sprint
problem.statement:
A runner has energy costs a_1 ... a_n for consecutive track segments.
All costs are positive.
The runner wants a contiguous segment whose total cost is at most k.
Find the maximum possible length of such a segment.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n positive integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum valid segment length.

diagnosis: After adding a[r], the window may exceed k by more than one left element.
Using if instead of while can leave the window invalid and still count it.
response.fix_explanation: Shrink in a while loop until the current window sum is at most k.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Budgeted Sprint
problem.statement:
A runner has energy costs a_1 ... a_n for consecutive track segments.
All costs are positive.
The runner wants a contiguous segment whose total cost is at most k.
Find the maximum possible length of such a segment.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n positive integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum valid segment length.

response.core_idea: Because all costs are positive, a window that is too expensive can only be fixed by moving its left end rightward.
algorithm_steps: Maintain a window [l, r] and its sum.
Extend r one step at a time.
While the sum exceeds k, subtract a[l] and increment l.
Track the maximum window length seen.
invariant_or_correctness: With positive values, once a window exceeds k, any longer window with the same left endpoint also exceeds k.
Therefore shrinking the left endpoint until the sum fits is both necessary and sufficient.
edge_cases: no segment longer than 1 is valid
response.explanation: Positivity is the key property. It makes two pointers exact rather than heuristic, because each pointer only moves forward.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Median Upgrade
problem.statement:
You are given an array of odd length n and an integer k.
In one move you may increase any array element by 1.
You may use at most k moves in total.
Maximize the median of the array after all moves.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum possible median.

failure_description.got_output: overflowed value leads to an impossible larger answer

diagnosis: The required increment total can exceed 2^31-1, so storing it in int overflows.
Once overflow occurs, the binary-search feasibility test becomes unreliable.
response.fix_explanation: Accumulate the required increment cost in long long and stop early if it exceeds k.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Median Upgrade
problem.statement:
You are given an array of odd length n and an integer k.
In one move you may increase any array element by 1.
You may use at most k moves in total.
Maximize the median of the array after all moves.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum possible median.

response.core_idea: Only the median and larger elements affect the final median. Binary-search the answer and check how many increments are needed to raise the upper half to that level.
algorithm_steps: Sort the array and locate the median index mid = n/2.
Binary-search a candidate median x.
For each candidate x, sum max(0, x-a[i]) for i from mid to n-1 and test whether the cost is at most k.
invariant_or_correctness: Elements left of the median never need to be increased, because they do not reduce the sorted median.
A candidate x is feasible exactly when the sorted upper half can all be made at least x within budget.
Feasibility is monotone in x, so binary search is valid.

complexity.time: O(n log n + n log k)

response.explanation: The feasibility test is monotone, which makes binary search natural. Sorting isolates the part of the array that can block the median from rising.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Previous Higher Beacon
problem.statement:
A sequence of towers has heights h_1 ... h_n.
For every position i, output the nearest index j < i such that h_j > h_i.
If no such index exists, output 0.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Second line: n integers h_1 ... h_n
problem.output_spec: Output n integers. The i-th integer is the answer for position i.

response.why_too_slow: In decreasing or equal-height patterns, the inner backward scan is nearly O(n) for every i.
response.optimization_idea: Use a monotonic decreasing stack so every index is pushed once and popped once.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Previous Higher Beacon
problem.statement:
A sequence of towers has heights h_1 ... h_n.
For every position i, output the nearest index j < i such that h_j > h_i.
If no such index exists, output 0.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Second line: n integers h_1 ... h_n
problem.output_spec: Output n integers. The i-th integer is the answer for position i.

diagnosis: The task asks for the nearest strictly greater height.
Equal heights must not remain on the stack, because they are not valid answers.
response.fix_explanation: Pop while the stack top is less than or equal to the current height.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Previous Higher Beacon
problem.statement:
A sequence of towers has heights h_1 ... h_n.
For every position i, output the nearest index j < i such that h_j > h_i.
If no such index exists, output 0.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Second line: n integers h_1 ... h_n
problem.output_spec: Output n integers. The i-th integer is the answer for position i.

response.core_idea: Keep a stack of indices with strictly decreasing heights. After removing all heights not greater than h_i, the top is the nearest previous higher tower.
algorithm_steps: Process towers from left to right.
While the stack top has height <= current height, pop it.
The remaining top, if any, is the nearest previous greater index.
Push the current index onto the stack.
invariant_or_correctness: The stack always stores heights in strictly decreasing order.
Any popped index is dominated by the current tower and can never answer a future query.
response.explanation: The monotonic stack deletes only dominated candidates. Each index is pushed and popped at most once.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Window Champion
problem.statement:
Given an array a_1 ... a_n and an integer k, output the maximum value
in every contiguous window of length k.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output n-k+1 integers: the maximum in each window from left to right.

response.why_too_slow: Each window recomputes its maximum from scratch.
response.optimization_idea: A monotonic deque stores only undominated indices of the current window, so the maximum is always at the front.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Window Champion
problem.statement:
Given an array a_1 ... a_n and an integer k, output the maximum value
in every contiguous window of length k.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output n-k+1 integers: the maximum in each window from left to right.

diagnosis: At window ending i, any index <= i-k is already outside the window and must be removed.
Using < i-k leaves one stale index alive for one extra step.
response.fix_explanation: Remove front indices while dq.front() <= i-k before reading the maximum.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Window Champion
problem.statement:
Given an array a_1 ... a_n and an integer k, output the maximum value
in every contiguous window of length k.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n k
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output n-k+1 integers: the maximum in each window from left to right.

response.core_idea: A deque of indices in decreasing value order stores exactly the candidates that can still become the maximum of the current or future windows.
algorithm_steps: Process indices from left to right.
Before inserting i, drop deque back indices with value <= a[i].
Push i, then remove the front index if it left the current window.
Once the first full window is formed, the deque front is the window maximum.
invariant_or_correctness: Deque values are kept in decreasing order, so the front is always the maximum candidate.
An index removed from the back is dominated by a later index with greater or equal value.
response.explanation: The deque only stores undominated indices inside the current window. That preserves exact maxima while keeping total work linear.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Hall Reservations
problem.statement:
There are n meetings. Meeting i occupies the half-open interval [l_i, r_i),
so a hall used by one meeting becomes available exactly at time r_i.
Find the minimum number of halls required to host all meetings.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Next n lines: l_i r_i
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the minimum number of halls.

response.why_too_slow: Scanning all rooms for every meeting can take O(n^2), and the code also depends on input order rather than event order.
response.optimization_idea: Sort starts and ends separately and sweep overlap count, releasing all meetings whose end time is <= the next start time.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Hall Reservations
problem.statement:
There are n meetings. Meeting i occupies the half-open interval [l_i, r_i),
so a hall used by one meeting becomes available exactly at time r_i.
Find the minimum number of halls required to host all meetings.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Next n lines: l_i r_i
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the minimum number of halls.

diagnosis: The intervals are half-open [l, r), so a meeting ending at time t does not overlap one starting at t.
Releasing halls only for end < start incorrectly treats touching intervals as overlapping.
response.fix_explanation: Release every meeting with end <= current start before allocating the new hall.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Hall Reservations
problem.statement:
There are n meetings. Meeting i occupies the half-open interval [l_i, r_i),
so a hall used by one meeting becomes available exactly at time r_i.
Find the minimum number of halls required to host all meetings.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Next n lines: l_i r_i
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the minimum number of halls.

response.core_idea: Sort all start times and end times separately. Sweep through starts while releasing every hall whose meeting has already ended.
algorithm_steps: Store all starts in one array and all ends in another.
Sort both arrays.
For each next start time, first advance the end pointer while end <= start.
After releasing finished meetings, occupy one hall for the new meeting and update the maximum active count.
invariant_or_correctness: active equals the number of meetings that have started but not yet ended.
Because intervals are half-open [l, r), meetings ending at time t do not overlap meetings starting at t.
response.explanation: The answer is the peak overlap count. Separating starts and ends makes that overlap easy to maintain with one sweep.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Signal Maze
problem.statement:
You are given an n x m grid containing:
'.' for an open cell, '#' for a wall, 'S' for the start, and 'T' for the target.
In one move you may go up, down, left, or right to another open cell.
Find the minimum number of moves from S to T, or output -1 if impossible.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next n lines: the grid
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the shortest path length, or -1.
problem.constraints:
1 <= n, m <= 1000
There is exactly one S and exactly one T.

diagnosis: The target cell T is not '.', but it is still traversable.
Filtering to '.' only makes the BFS unable to enter the target.
response.fix_explanation: Treat every non-wall cell as traversable, including both S and T.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Signal Maze
problem.statement:
You are given an n x m grid containing:
'.' for an open cell, '#' for a wall, 'S' for the start, and 'T' for the target.
In one move you may go up, down, left, or right to another open cell.
Find the minimum number of moves from S to T, or output -1 if impossible.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next n lines: the grid
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the shortest path length, or -1.
problem.constraints:
1 <= n, m <= 1000
There is exactly one S and exactly one T.

response.core_idea: The grid is an unweighted graph, so BFS from S visits cells in increasing distance order.
algorithm_steps: Locate S and T, and initialize all distances to -1.
Run BFS from S over the four directions, ignoring walls and already visited cells.
The first distance assigned to T is the shortest path length.
invariant_or_correctness: BFS expands cells layer by layer by move count.
When a cell is first reached, that path uses the minimum possible number of moves.
response.explanation: All moves cost one, so standard BFS already guarantees shortest paths in this grid.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Semester Signals
problem.statement:
There are n courses and m prerequisite relations u -> v, meaning course u
must be completed before course v.
In one semester you may take any number of courses whose prerequisites are already completed.
Find the minimum number of semesters needed to finish all courses.
If the prerequisite graph contains a cycle, output -1.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next m lines: u v
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the minimum number of semesters, or -1 if impossible.

diagnosis: Kahn's algorithm only processes nodes reachable from the zero-indegree frontier.
If a cycle exists, some nodes never enter the queue, so printing the partial answer is invalid.
response.fix_explanation: Count processed nodes and output -1 whenever processed != n.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Semester Signals
problem.statement:
There are n courses and m prerequisite relations u -> v, meaning course u
must be completed before course v.
In one semester you may take any number of courses whose prerequisites are already completed.
Find the minimum number of semesters needed to finish all courses.
If the prerequisite graph contains a cycle, output -1.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next m lines: u v
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the minimum number of semesters, or -1 if impossible.

response.core_idea: Perform Kahn's topological sort. Along that order, dp[v] is the earliest semester in which v can be taken.
algorithm_steps: Build indegrees and adjacency lists.
Initialize zero-indegree courses with dp = 1 and push them into the queue.
Pop u, relax each edge u -> v with dp[v] = max(dp[v], dp[u] + 1), and push v when its indegree becomes zero.
If not all nodes are processed, a cycle exists.
invariant_or_correctness: A course can only be taken after all predecessors are processed, which is exactly what zero indegree means in Kahn's algorithm.
dp[v] tracks the longest prerequisite chain ending at v, so it equals the earliest feasible semester for v.
response.explanation: This is longest path on a DAG plus cycle detection. Kahn's algorithm handles both in one pass.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Cut Value Tree
problem.statement:
You are given a tree with n vertices.
If you remove one edge, the tree splits into two connected components of sizes x and n-x.
The value of that cut is x * (n - x).
Find the maximum possible cut value over all edges.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Next n-1 lines: u v
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum cut value.

response.why_too_slow: It recomputes a graph traversal for every edge, which is quadratic on a tree.
response.optimization_idea: Root the tree once, compute subtree sizes once, and evaluate every cut using sub[u] * (n-sub[u]).

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Cut Value Tree
problem.statement:
You are given a tree with n vertices.
If you remove one edge, the tree splits into two connected components of sizes x and n-x.
The value of that cut is x * (n - x).
Find the maximum possible cut value over all edges.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Next n-1 lines: u v
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum cut value.

response.core_idea: For an edge connecting a node's subtree to its parent, the cut sizes are exactly subtree_size and n-subtree_size.
algorithm_steps: Root the tree at 1 and build a parent array plus traversal order.
Process nodes in reverse order to compute subtree sizes.
For every non-root node u, removing the edge to its parent creates parts of sizes sub[u] and n-sub[u].
Track the maximum product.
invariant_or_correctness: Every edge corresponds to exactly one child subtree in the rooted tree.
The subtree size of that child is one component size, and the rest of the tree is the other component.
response.explanation: The problem only asks about component sizes after one cut, so subtree sizes are sufficient. No per-edge recomputation is needed.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Alliance Desk
problem.statement:
There are n players, initially each in their own alliance.
Process q operations:
- union a b : merge the alliances containing a and b
- size x : output the size of the alliance containing x
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Next q lines: either "union a b" or "size x"
problem.output_spec: For every size query, output one integer.

samples.input:
5 6
size 1
union 1 2
size 2
union 2 3
union 4 5
size 1

response.core_idea: Use a disjoint-set union structure with union by size and path compression.
algorithm_steps: Initialize each node as its own parent with component size 1.
For union a b, merge the two roots if distinct.
For size x, find the root of x and print its component size.
invariant_or_correctness: Each set is represented by one root; size[root] stores the component size.
Path compression preserves correctness while making future finds faster.
response.explanation: This is the standard DSU use case. The only dynamic information needed is component membership and root size.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Courier Paths
problem.statement:
You are given an undirected weighted graph with n vertices and m edges.
Find the length of the shortest path from vertex 1 to vertex n.
If there is no path, output -1.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next m lines: u v w
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the shortest path length from 1 to n, or -1.

response.why_too_slow: Bellman-Ford is correct but far too slow for sparse non-negative graphs with up to 2e5 edges.
response.optimization_idea: Use Dijkstra with a min-priority queue because all weights are non-negative.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Courier Paths
problem.statement:
You are given an undirected weighted graph with n vertices and m edges.
Find the length of the shortest path from vertex 1 to vertex n.
If there is no path, output -1.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next m lines: u v w
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the shortest path length from 1 to n, or -1.

diagnosis: Shortest-path distances can exceed the 32-bit range when many large edges are added.
Both the graph weights and the distance array must use long long.
response.fix_explanation: Store weights and distances as long long and keep the priority queue keyed by long long distances.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Courier Paths
problem.statement:
You are given an undirected weighted graph with n vertices and m edges.
Find the length of the shortest path from vertex 1 to vertex n.
If there is no path, output -1.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next m lines: u v w
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the shortest path length from 1 to n, or -1.

response.core_idea: All edge weights are non-negative, so Dijkstra from node 1 gives the shortest distances.
algorithm_steps: Build the weighted adjacency list.
Initialize dist[1] = 0 and all others to INF.
Run Dijkstra with a min-priority queue.
Output dist[n], or -1 if it was never improved.
invariant_or_correctness: Whenever a node is popped with its current best distance, that distance is final under non-negative weights.
Relaxing outgoing edges preserves shortest-known upper bounds on all nodes.
edge_cases: large distance sums requiring long long
response.explanation: The graph is sparse and weights are non-negative, so Dijkstra is the standard scalable choice.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Score Ledger
problem.statement:
An array of n scores is initially all zero.
Process q operations:
- add i x : add x to position i
- sum l r : output the sum of positions l through r
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Next q lines: either "add i x" or "sum l r"
problem.output_spec: For every sum query, output one integer.

samples.input:
5 6
add 2 4
add 5 7
sum 1 5
add 2 -1
sum 2 4
sum 5 5

response.why_too_slow: Range sums linearly scan the interval, so many large queries are too expensive.
response.optimization_idea: Fenwick tree gives both point additions and prefix sums in O(log n), so each range sum is two prefix queries.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Score Ledger
problem.statement:
An array of n scores is initially all zero.
Process q operations:
- add i x : add x to position i
- sum l r : output the sum of positions l through r
problem.input_spec:
First line: n q
Next q lines: either "add i x" or "sum l r"
problem.output_spec: For every sum query, output one integer.

samples.input:
5 6
add 2 4
add 5 7
sum 1 5
add 2 -1
sum 2 4
sum 5 5

response.core_idea: A Fenwick tree supports point additions and prefix sums, so a range sum is prefix(r) - prefix(l-1).
algorithm_steps: Store the array implicitly inside a Fenwick tree.
For add i x, update Fenwick index i by x.
For sum l r, return prefix(r) - prefix(l-1).
invariant_or_correctness: Fenwick nodes store disjoint suffixes of prefixes, so prefix sums decompose exactly into O(log n) nodes.
Point updates touch exactly the Fenwick nodes whose covered ranges include the updated index.
response.explanation: Fenwick is the minimal data structure here: easier than a segment tree and fast enough for large online updates plus queries.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Parcel Budget
problem.statement:
There are n parcels. Parcel i has weight w_i and value v_i.
You may take each parcel at most once.
Find the maximum total value of chosen parcels whose total weight is at most W.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n W
Next n lines: w_i v_i
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum achievable total value.

response.core_idea: This is 0/1 knapsack. Use dp[c] = best value achievable with capacity c after processing some prefix of items.
algorithm_steps: Initialize dp[0..W] = 0.
For each item (weight, value), iterate capacities from W down to weight.
Transition dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-weight] + value).
invariant_or_correctness: Before processing an item, dp[c] represents the best value using earlier items only.
Descending capacity order ensures the same item is not reused within one iteration.
response.explanation: The descending loop order is the crucial 0/1 detail. It preserves the one-use constraint while keeping memory one-dimensional.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Blocked Orchard
problem.statement:
You are given an n x m grid with open cells '.' and blocked cells '#'.
Starting at (1, 1), you may move only right or down.
Count the number of ways to reach (n, m) without entering blocked cells.
Output the answer modulo 1,000,000,007.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next n lines: the grid
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the number of valid paths modulo 1,000,000,007.
problem.constraints:
1 <= n, m <= 2000
The cell (1, 1) and (n, m) may be blocked.

response.core_idea: Let dp[i][j] be the number of ways to reach cell (i, j). Open cells inherit paths from above and left.
algorithm_steps: If the start cell is blocked, answer 0 immediately.
Iterate the grid row by row.
For each open cell, add the number of ways from the top and left neighbors modulo 1e9+7.
invariant_or_correctness: Any valid path to an open cell must arrive from exactly one of the two allowed predecessor cells.
Blocked cells contribute zero paths and break transitions cleanly.
response.explanation: The move set is acyclic and local, so a simple forward DP is enough.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Tower Parade
problem.statement:
There are n towers. Tower i has width w_i and height h_i.
You want the longest sequence of distinct towers such that both width and height
are strictly increasing from one chosen tower to the next.
Output the maximum possible length.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n
Next n lines: w_i h_i
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum chain length.

response.core_idea: Sort by width ascending and by height descending on equal widths. Then the answer is the LIS length on heights.
algorithm_steps: Sort pairs by (width asc, height desc).
Scan the sorted list and maintain the LIS tails array on heights.
Use lower_bound for strict increasing LIS on the transformed sequence.
invariant_or_correctness: Descending heights on equal widths prevent choosing two towers with the same width in the LIS step.
The LIS tails invariant guarantees tails[len-1] is the minimum possible last height of an increasing chain of length len.
response.explanation: This is the standard 2D-chain reduction to LIS. The tie-breaking on equal widths is what makes the reduction correct.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Panel Switches
problem.statement:
There are m bulbs labeled 0 to m-1, initially all off.
There are n switches. Switch i has a cost c_i and turns on a fixed subset of bulbs.
Each switch may be used at most once, and once a bulb is on it stays on.
Find the minimum total cost needed to turn on all bulbs, or output -1 if impossible.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next n lines: c_i t_i b_1 ... b_t
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the minimum total cost, or -1.

response.core_idea: Represent the set of bulbs already turned on as a bitmask. DP over masks gives the minimum cost to reach every coverage state.
algorithm_steps: Convert every switch's bulb list into a bitmask.
Initialize dp[0] = 0 and all other masks to INF.
For each switch, try taking it from every current mask and relax dp[mask | switch_mask].
invariant_or_correctness: dp[mask] is the minimum cost to achieve the covered-bulb set mask after processing some prefix of switches.
Using or skipping the next switch preserves optimal substructure because each switch can be used at most once.
response.explanation: The state space is small enough because m <= 20. The bitmask DP is exact and easy to verify.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Warehouse Lanes
problem.statement:
A lane has n containers with weights a_1 ... a_n.
A segment is stable if the difference between its maximum and minimum weight is at most d.
Find the maximum length of a stable contiguous segment.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n d
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum stable segment length.

response.core_idea: Maintain the current window's minimum and maximum with two deques while expanding the right pointer.
algorithm_steps: Use one deque increasing by value for minima and one decreasing for maxima.
Extend the right endpoint and update both deques.
While max-min > d, move the left endpoint right and discard expired indices.
Track the best window length.
invariant_or_correctness: The min deque front is always the minimum in the window; the max deque front is always the maximum.
A window is stable exactly when those two front values differ by at most d.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Mirror Prefix Search
problem.statement:
Given a pattern p and a text t, output all starting positions where p occurs in t.
Positions are 1-indexed.
problem.input_spec:
First line: p
Second line: t
problem.output_spec: First output c, then on the next line output all occurrence positions in increasing order.
problem.constraints:
1 <= |p|, |t| <= 2 * 10^5
Both strings consist of lowercase English letters.

response.core_idea: Form the string p + '#' + t and compute its Z-array. A match starts wherever the Z-value reaches |p| inside the text part.
algorithm_steps: Concatenate pattern, separator, and text.
Compute the Z-array in linear time.
For each text position, if Z there is at least |p|, record the corresponding 1-indexed start.
invariant_or_correctness: Z[i] is the longest prefix length matching the suffix starting at i.
Therefore Z[i] >= |p| exactly when the pattern matches at that shifted text position.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Residue Triples
problem.statement:
You are given n integers and a modulus m.
Count the number of triples (i, j, k) with i < j < k such that
(a_i + a_j + a_k) mod m = 0.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Second line: n integers a_1 ... a_n
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the number of valid triples.

response.core_idea: Count how many numbers fall into each residue class modulo m, then enumerate residue triples whose sum is 0 mod m and multiply by the corresponding combination counts.
algorithm_steps: Normalize every value to a residue in [0, m-1] and build freq[r].
Enumerate residue choices r1 <= r2 <= r3 with (r1+r2+r3) mod m = 0.
For each pattern, add the right combinatorial term based on how many residues are equal.
invariant_or_correctness: Only residue classes matter for divisibility by m.
Once the residue multiset is fixed, the number of index triples depends only on how many residues are equal.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Fire Stations
problem.statement:
You are given an n x m city map with '.' empty roads, '#' blocked cells, and 'F' fire stations.
For each non-blocked cell, compute the distance to the nearest fire station using 4-direction moves.
Distances for blocked cells should remain -1.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m
Next n lines: the grid
problem.output_spec: Output an n x m matrix of integers.
problem.constraints:
1 <= n, m <= 1000
There is at least one fire station.

response.core_idea: Treat every fire station as a BFS source with distance 0 and propagate outward once.
algorithm_steps: Initialize the answer matrix with -1 for blocked cells and INF for open cells.
Push all fire-station cells into the queue with distance 0.
Run BFS in four directions and relax neighbors the first time they are seen.
invariant_or_correctness: Multi-source BFS processes all cells in order of distance from the nearest source.
The first assigned distance to a cell is therefore its minimum distance to any station.
edge_cases: multiple stations adjacent to the same cell

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Ancestor Budget
problem.statement:
The tree is rooted at node 1. Node i has a positive cost c_i.
Choose a connected set of nodes that must contain the root.
The total cost of chosen nodes must not exceed B.
Maximize the number of chosen nodes.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n B
Second line: c_1 ... c_n
Next n-1 lines: u v
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum number of chosen nodes.

response.core_idea: Use subtree knapsack DP. For each node, dp_u[c] stores the maximum number of chosen nodes in u's subtree with total cost c, under the condition that the chosen set is connected and contains u.
algorithm_steps: Root the tree at 1.
Initialize dp_u[c_u] = 1 for each node u, and impossible elsewhere.
Merge each child v into u with a knapsack convolution over budgets.
The final answer is max dp_root[c] for c <= B.
data_structures: DP tables by subtree and budget
invariant_or_correctness: Connectivity is preserved because child states are only merged into a parent state that already includes the parent.
Every feasible connected rooted selection appears in exactly one merge outcome.
edge_cases: many children competing for the same budget

complexity.time: O(n * B^2) in the straightforward implementation

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Broken Network
problem.statement:
There is an undirected graph with n vertices and m initial edges.
Then q operations follow:
- remove u v : delete that edge from the graph
- ask u v : answer whether u and v are connected at that moment
Every removed edge is guaranteed to exist when removed.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m q
Next m lines: initial edges u v
Next q lines: operations
problem.output_spec: For every ask operation, output YES or NO.

response.core_idea: Dynamic deletions are hard online for DSU, so process operations in reverse where removals become insertions.
algorithm_steps: Mark every edge that will eventually be removed.
Build a DSU with only the edges that survive all forward operations.
Process operations backward: reverse ask as ask, reverse remove as add edge.
Record answers in reverse order and reverse them at the end.
invariant_or_correctness: When running backward, the DSU state matches exactly the graph state after the corresponding prefix of reversed operations.
Because DSU supports insertions but not deletions, reversing time makes the data structure compatible with the operation sequence.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Festival Squares
problem.statement:
You are given an n x m grid of non-negative values and an integer K.
Find the largest side length L such that there exists an L x L square whose sum is at most K.
problem.input_spec:
First line: n m K
Next n lines: m non-negative integers each
problem.output_spec: Output one integer: the maximum valid side length.

response.core_idea: Use a 2D prefix sum to query any square sum in O(1), then binary-search the side length.
algorithm_steps: Build the 2D prefix-sum matrix.
Binary-search a candidate side length L.
For each top-left corner, query the L x L sum in O(1); if any is <= K, L is feasible.
invariant_or_correctness: 2D prefix sums return the exact sum of any axis-aligned square by inclusion-exclusion.
If a side length L is feasible, every smaller side length is also feasible because all grid values are non-negative.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.

problem.title: Echo Equality
problem.statement:
You are given a string s and q queries.
Each query contains l_1, r_1, l_2, r_2.
Determine whether substring s[l_1..r_1] is equal to substring s[l_2..r_2].
problem.input_spec:
First line: s
Second line: q
Next q lines: l_1 r_1 l_2 r_2
problem.output_spec: For every query, output YES or NO.

response.core_idea: Precompute polynomial rolling hashes and powers. Then every substring hash is O(1), so substring-equality queries become hash comparisons.
algorithm_steps: Build prefix hashes and base powers for the string.
Define a function that returns the normalized hash of any substring.
For each query, if the substring lengths differ answer NO; otherwise compare their hashes.
invariant_or_correctness: Prefix hashes allow any substring hash to be isolated by subtraction and scaling.
Equal-length substrings with equal normalized hashes are treated as equal under the chosen hash scheme.

verification_reserved.notes: Custom-authored item. Reserved for future compile/checker verification.
